description: P1.3 publisher/template baseline whitelist. Each rule demotes a high finding to medium when the matched signal class is known to fire on benign, publisher-production or template-level patterns. Findings are never dropped; the demotion is recorded in raw.publisher_baseline. Rules are applied after the evidence recalibration and cluster demotion layers. An optional 'publisher' token restricts a rule to documents from that publisher (matched against the normalized publisher name derived from the document DOI prefix or metadata); omit it for global rules.

rules.rationale: The same DOI re-listed with a conflicting year or surname is almost always a version/reprint difference (preprint vs VoR, corrected edition) or citation-style variance, not fabricated referencing; virtually every legitimate paper shows at least one.

Byte-identical image files reused across extractions are typically publisher production artifacts (journal logos, decorative banners, graphical-abstract boilerplate) or legitimately repeated schematic panels, not manipulated figures.

Local SIFT matches with moderate RANSAC inlier counts fire on naturally similar content across figures of legitimate biomedical papers (same staining, same microscope setup, repeated scale bars); the signal merits review but is not high-priority on its own.

Near-identical local texture cells recur in legitimate multi-panel microscopy/histology figures because backgrounds, staining textures and blank regions are naturally shared across panels; standalone texture overlap is a review cue, not a high-severity indicator.
Panel-to-panel SIFT matches inside one figure fire on legitimate composites (time courses, replicate conditions, split views of the same sample) where panels are expected to be near-identical.

Strong SSIM/pHash matches between panels of the same multi-panel figure are expected in normal medical imaging figures (serial sections, time series, replicates); contour-based panel duplication is inherently noisy on such composites.
Whole-panel pHash matches in multi-panel medical figures are commonly legitimate (identical backgrounds, repeated template panels); same reasoning as panel_duplicate.

image_dup high findings are either bridge mirrors of the image_forensics local-match signals above or pHash near-duplicates between naturally similar panels; they carry no independent evidence beyond the demoted forensics classes.
Page/region raster tiles with near-identical hashes are dominated by shared page furniture (headers, footers, watermark bands, blank margins) from the publisher template, not duplicated content.

Near-duplicate rows are expected in structured legitimate tables (time courses, dose series, per-patient listings with repetitive unit columns); row-level similarity alone is weak evidence of copying.
Exact duplicate row groups occur in legitimate tables with repeated control conditions or symmetric layouts; without corroborating signals they do not warrant high severity.
Row patterns reused across tables of the same paper usually reflect a shared table template or repeated header/control rows rather than cross-table data copying.
